planMarkdown:
Bot: Motivation & Life Tips Telegram Bot (Production-Ready Plan)

Summary:
A Telegram bot that delivers motivational messages and practical life tips in a personalized, safe, and reliable way. Users can request on-demand motivation, choose topics (habits, productivity, stress, relationships, fitness, confidence), set a daily schedule, and track streaks and preferences. The system is built as a single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence and optional filesystem/object storage for any admin-imported content assets.

The bot emphasizes user control (frequency, quiet hours, opt-out), content quality (curated tips + admin moderation), and operational robustness (rate limiting, idempotent scheduling, error handling, observability). It also supports an admin workflow for adding, reviewing, and segmenting content so the bot remains fresh without redeployments.

Product goals:
Primary user value
1) Provide fast, helpful motivation: On-demand messages that feel relevant to the user’s moment and preferences.
2) Build positive routines: Daily scheduled tips and reminders that users can tailor (time, frequency, topics).
3) Encourage small actions: Each message can include a lightweight prompt (one-minute task, reflection question) to convert motivation into behavior.

Core features
1) On-demand motivation: A single command or button produces a message immediately.
2) Topic selection: Users choose categories and can exclude topics.
3) Scheduling: Daily (or multiple times/day) delivery with quiet hours.
4) Streaks and light tracking: Track consecutive days interacted with, and optionally simple “did it” confirmations.
5) Personalization: Basic preference-based selection plus rotation to avoid repetition.
6) Safety and boundaries: Non-medical disclaimers, crisis guidance language, and content filters for sensitive topics.

Admin and content goals
1) Curated content library: Store tips/quotes/prompts with metadata, topics, language, tone, and difficulty.
2) Moderation and iteration: Admin review workflow and quick disabling of problematic content.
3) Analytics-lite: Aggregate counts (deliveries, opt-outs, topic popularity) without collecting unnecessary personal data.

Out of scope (initially)
1) Deep mental-health counseling or diagnostic advice.
2) Heavy gamification or social leaderboards that could harm privacy.
3) Complex NLP personalization; keep it rule-based for predictability.

High-level architecture:
High-level design (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram update handling: grammY receives updates via webhook or long polling. The bot routes updates through middleware for logging, rate limiting, and user session loading.
2) Core modules:
   1) Conversation/UI module: Menus and flows for onboarding, topic selection, scheduling, and feedback.
   2) Content selection engine: Picks the next tip based on user preferences, recent history, and content availability.
   3) Scheduler: A periodic in-process job that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and sends messages. It must be idempotent and safe across retries.
   4) Admin tools: Commands for ingesting content, approving content, and viewing basic stats.
   5) Safety and policy layer: Filters and disclaimers, plus a simple “not a substitute for professional help” footer for certain categories.

Request path and middleware
1) Inbound updates go through:
   1) Correlation ID assignment for logs.
   2) Basic rate limiting per user/chat to reduce spam and accidental loops.
   3) User record loading/creation in MongoDB.
   4) Locale and preference resolution.
   5) Command and callback routing.
2) Outbound messages go through:
   1) Send wrapper with retries for transient errors.
   2) Telegram error classification (blocked bot, chat not found) to auto-disable scheduling for that chat.

Scheduling approach (no extra infra)
1) A timer (for example every 30–60 seconds) runs a “due deliveries” scan.
2) It finds user schedules whose nextDeliveryAt is in the past, attempts to send, and then updates nextDeliveryAt.
3) Idempotency: Each scheduled send is recorded as a delivery event with a unique key (userId + scheduleId + deliveryWindow) so that restarts do not create duplicates.
4) Backoff: If Telegram fails due to rate limits or transient errors, reschedule with a short delay and track attempt counts.

Reliability boundaries
1) The service remains single-process as required; durability comes from MongoDB.
2) All “state transitions” (e.g., schedule advanced, content marked as used) are persisted so a restart continues correctly.
3) If MongoDB is down, the bot can still respond minimally (inform user) but should avoid losing scheduling state; it can temporarily disable scheduled sends until persistence is restored.

Data model:
User collection
1) userId: Telegram user ID
2) chatId: chat to deliver messages (private chat default)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) status: active, paused, blocked
5) locale: e.g., en
6) preferences:
   1) topicsIncluded: list
   2) topicsExcluded: list
   3) tone: uplifting, practical, tough-love (optional)
   4) messageLength: short, medium (optional)
7) quietHours:
   1) startLocalTime, endLocalTime
   2) timezoneOffsetMinutes or inferred timezone mode
8) streak:
   1) currentCount
   2) lastInteractionDate (date only in user’s local notion)
9) deliveryHistory (lightweight pointers): lastNContentIds or a rolling hash to reduce repetition

Schedules collection
1) scheduleId
2) userId, chatId
3) enabled: boolean
4) frequency:
   1) dailyAtLocalTime or multipleTimes array
   2) daysOfWeek optional
5) nextDeliveryAt: UTC timestamp
6) lastDeliveredAt
7) lastDeliveryContentId
8) failureState:
   1) consecutiveFailures
   2) lastFailureReason

Content collection
1) contentId
2) type: tip, quote, prompt, micro-challenge
3) text: primary message
4) topics: list
5) tone, length
6) language
7) safetyTags: e.g., mental-health-sensitive, medical
8) status: draft, approved, disabled
9) author/source metadata (optional) for attribution
10) createdAt, updatedAt

DeliveryEvents collection (for idempotency and audit)
1) eventId
2) userId, scheduleId
3) deliveryKey: unique string
4) contentId
5) status: sent, failed, skipped
6) attemptedAt, sentAt
7) telegramMessageId (if sent)

Feedback collection (optional but useful)
1) userId, contentId
2) rating: like, dislike
3) freeformReason (optional)
4) createdAt

Admin collection (or config)
1) adminUserIds list in environment/config
2) content import logs and actions (optional).

Key flows:
Onboarding flow
1) User starts with /start.
2) Bot explains what it does, asks for topic preferences (buttons).
3) Bot asks if user wants daily motivation and at what time.
4) Bot confirms quiet hours default and how to change later.
5) Bot sends a sample message and offers “More like this / Less like this” feedback.

On-demand motivation
1) User runs /motivate or taps “Motivate me”.
2) Selection engine filters approved content by language, included/excluded topics, and tone.
3) De-duplication: avoid last N items delivered to that user.
4) Bot sends message with quick feedback buttons:
   1) Another one
   2) Save
   3) Not for me
   4) Change topics

Daily scheduled delivery
1) Scheduler finds due schedules.
2) For each schedule:
   1) Check user status active.
   2) Check quiet hours; if within quiet hours, move nextDeliveryAt to end of quiet window.
   3) Select content and create a DeliveryEvent with unique deliveryKey.
   4) Send message; on success, mark event sent and advance schedule.
   5) On known hard failures (bot blocked), set user status blocked and disable schedule.

Streak update
1) On any meaningful interaction (requesting a tip, giving feedback), update lastInteractionDate.
2) If lastInteractionDate is yesterday (user-local), increment streak; otherwise reset to 1.
3) Optionally show streak milestones sparingly to avoid annoyance.

Feedback loop
1) Like: increases weight for that topic/tone and can add contentId to a “favorites” list.
2) Dislike: mark contentId as “avoid for this user” and optionally reduce topic weight.
3) If user repeatedly dislikes a topic, prompt to adjust preferences.

Admin content management
1) Admin adds content via command (text pasted or uploaded file parsed externally by admin and inserted manually).
2) Content enters draft status.
3) Admin reviews and approves; only approved content is eligible.
4) Admin can disable content immediately if issues arise.
5) Admin can request basic stats: deliveries today, top topics, failure counts.

Safety handling
1) If user asks for mental health crisis help, bot responds with a safe, non-clinical message encouraging contacting local emergency services or trusted contacts.
2) For sensitive tags, append a brief disclaimer and avoid prescriptive medical claims.
3) Provide /stop and /pause for control, always respected.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js using ESM modules.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.
3) A single Node.js service/process for all update handling and scheduling.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for user profiles, schedules, content library, delivery events, and feedback.
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (e.g., recent rate-limit buckets, short-lived session hints), never as the source of truth.

Scheduling
1) In-process interval-based scheduler that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and updates schedule state transactionally where possible.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Storage for assets (optional)
1) If later supporting images/audio: store files in simple filesystem storage or object storage.
2) Store only file references in MongoDB.

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) MongoDB connection string and admin user IDs from environment variables.
3) Feature flags via environment variables (e.g., enable daily tips, enable feedback collection).

Deployment
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Use webhook mode if the environment supports inbound HTTPS, otherwise long polling.
3) Graceful shutdown handling to finish in-flight sends and stop scheduler cleanly.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent scheduling via DeliveryEvents so restarts do not duplicate scheduled messages.
2) Defensive sending: classify Telegram API errors, disable schedules for blocked chats, and retry only when appropriate.
3) Data integrity: ensure schedule updates and delivery events are consistent; prefer atomic updates per schedule.

Performance
1) Batch due-schedule queries and limit concurrency of outbound sends to respect Telegram limits.
2) Use simple caching for content counts or topic lists, but rely on MongoDB as source of truth.

Security and privacy
1) Collect minimal user data: Telegram IDs, preferences, schedule settings, and delivery history.
2) Provide /delete to remove user data and disable schedules.
3) Avoid storing message text from users unless necessary for a feature; keep feedback structured.
4) Admin commands restricted by admin user IDs.

Observability
1) Structured logs for updates, sends, failures, and scheduler cycles.
2) Basic metrics counters (even if only logged): sends attempted, sends succeeded, failures by type, opt-outs.
3) Error tracking integration can be added conceptually, but keep implementation within the single service boundaries.

Content quality and compliance
1) Maintain an approval workflow for new content.
2) Add guardrails: no hate, harassment, medical claims, or targeted personal advice.
3) Localization-ready content fields to allow adding more languages later.

User experience
1) Respect quiet hours and allow easy pause/stop.
2) Keep messages short by default; allow users to opt into longer tips.
3) Use buttons for common actions to reduce typing.

Operational playbook
1) Back up MongoDB regularly.
2) Have an emergency kill switch to disable scheduled sends (environment flag) if needed.
3) Provide admin commands to pause all schedules or disable a content category quickly.

Bot commands:
• /start — Begin onboarding and set initial preferences for motivation topics and delivery schedule. (usage: /start) [user]
• /motivate — Get an instant motivational message or practical life tip based on your preferences. (usage: /motivate) [user]
• /topics — Choose which topics you want more or less of (habits, productivity, stress, confidence, etc.). (usage: /topics) [user]
• /schedule — Configure daily delivery time and frequency, and enable/disable scheduled messages. (usage: /schedule) [user]
• /pause — Pause scheduled motivation without deleting settings. (usage: /pause) [user]
• /resume — Resume scheduled motivation after pausing. (usage: /resume) [user]
• /quiet — Set quiet hours when the bot should not send scheduled messages. (usage: /quiet) [user]
• /streak — View your current engagement streak and recent activity. (usage: /streak) [user]
• /feedback — Adjust what you receive by rating the last message or setting preferences. (usage: /feedback) [user]
• /help — Show help, tips on using the bot, and safety guidance for sensitive situations. (usage: /help) [user]
• /stop — Stop all scheduled messages and opt out. (usage: /stop) [user]
• /delete — Delete your stored data (preferences, schedules, history) and disable the bot for you. (usage: /delete) [user]
• /adminaddcontent — Add a new motivational tip/quote/prompt to the content library (admin only). (usage: /adminaddcontent) [admin]
• /adminreview — List draft content awaiting approval and approve/disable items (admin only). (usage: /adminreview) [admin]
• /adminstats — View basic operational stats such as sends today and failure counts (admin only). (usage: /adminstats) [admin]
• /adminpauseall — Emergency switch to pause all scheduled deliveries (admin only). (usage: /adminpauseall) [admin]

DOCS.md.content:
This bot provides motivational messages and practical life tips tailored to a user’s chosen categories, with optional daily reminders.

Setup
1) Install dependencies
Run: npm run install:root

2) Configure environment
Copy .env.sample to .env and set at least TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.

3) Run
Development: npm run dev
Production: npm run build then npm start

Public commands
1) /start
Starts onboarding.
It asks you to pick categories (multi-select) and whether you want daily reminders.

2) /help
Shows available commands and short examples.

3) /motivate
Generates a short motivational message tailored to your selected categories and recent mood (if set).
Buttons: Save, Another, Change categories.

4) /tip
Generates a practical, concise tip tailored to your categories.
It includes 2–4 steps.
Buttons: Save, Another, Change categories.

5) /mood
Lets you select a mood (Great, Okay, Stressed, Sad, Unmotivated, Angry, Anxious).
The bot stores your latest mood and replies with a message in that tone.

6) /categories
Shows your current selected categories and lets you toggle them.

7) /remind
Configures reminders.
Flow: enter time (HH:MM) then timezone (IANA like Europe/London, default UTC), then reminder type (Motivation, Tip, Mixed).

8) /remind_off
Turns off reminders.

9) /save
Saves the last delivered motivation/tip item.

10) /favorites
Lists your saved items with pagination.
Each item has a Remove button.

11) /remove_favorite
If invoked as a command, it guides you to use /favorites for removal.

12) /history
Shows your last 10 delivered items with a short snippet and timestamp.

13) /reset
Clears the conversation memory used for AI context (if DB is enabled). It does not change your preferences.

14) /admin_status (admin-only)
Shows diagnostics: uptime, DB connected, reminders enabled count, last reminder loop run time, env presence booleans.
If BOT_ADMINS is not set, the command is disabled.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Telegram bot token.

2) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT (required for AI)
Base URL for CookMyBots AI Gateway. Example: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai

3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY (required for AI)
API key for the CookMyBots AI Gateway.

4) MONGODB_URI (optional but recommended)
Enables stored preferences, reminders, favorites, and history.
If not set, the bot runs in stateless mode with defaults and disables persistence features.

5) BOT_ADMINS (optional)
Comma-separated Telegram user IDs allowed to run /admin_status.
If missing, admin commands are disabled.

Reminder behavior
The reminder loop runs every 60 seconds in the same process.
It checks each reminders-enabled user’s local time based on their configured timezone.
It sends once per day per scheduled minute using a lastReminderSentAt field to prevent duplicates.

AI safety notes
The bot is supportive and practical.
It avoids moralizing, shame, and absolute claims.
It does not provide medical or legal advice.
If the user expresses potential self-harm or severe crisis, the bot responds with a supportive message encouraging contacting local emergency services or a trusted person.

Troubleshooting
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN missing
The bot exits with a clear message. Set it and restart.

2) Mongo not configured
Preferences/reminders/favorites/history are disabled. The bot will tell the user when they try to use those features.

3) AI not configured
/motivate and /tip will explain that AI keys are missing.

4) Conflicting polling instances
If Telegram returns a 409 conflict, the bot backs off and retries automatically.

README.md.content:
This repo contains a Telegram bot (grammY) that delivers motivational messages and practical life tips.

Features
1) Personalized categories (multi-select)
2) /motivate and /tip generation via CookMyBots AI Gateway
3) Mood selection (/mood) to adjust tone
4) Favorites (save/remove + pagination)
5) History (last 10 delivered items)
6) Optional daily reminders (single-process polling loop)
7) Admin diagnostics (/admin_status)

Architecture
1) src/index.js boots config, starts the bot via long polling, and starts the reminders loop.
2) src/bot.js wires commands first, then the agent features (no catch-all chat).
3) src/commands/* contains public commands.
4) src/features/* contains callback handlers and reminders loop.
5) src/lib/* contains config, DB, AI gateway client, logging helpers.

Setup
1) Install
   npm run install:root

2) Configure env
   Copy .env.sample to .env and fill in values.

Required
- TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN

Required for AI generation
- COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT (base URL, example: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai)
- COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY

Optional but recommended (enables preferences, reminders, favorites, history)
- MONGODB_URI

Optional admin
- BOT_ADMINS (comma-separated Telegram user IDs)

Run
1) Dev
   npm run dev

2) Prod
   npm run build
   npm start

Commands
1) /start
   Starts onboarding: pick categories, then optionally configure reminders.

2) /help
   Shows command list and examples.

3) /motivate
   Generates a short motivational message tailored to your categories and mood. Includes buttons Save, Another, Change categories.

4) /tip
   Generates a practical tip with 2–4 steps tailored to your categories. Includes buttons Save, Another, Change categories.

5) /mood
   Pick your current mood via buttons; bot stores it and responds in that tone.

6) /categories
   View and toggle category preferences.

7) /remind
   Configure reminders: time (HH:MM), timezone (IANA, defaults to UTC), and reminder type (Motivation/Tip/Mixed).

8) /remind_off
   Disable reminders.

9) /save
   Saves the last delivered item.

10) /favorites
   Lists saved items with pagination and Remove buttons.

11) /remove_favorite
   Guides you to remove items via /favorites.

12) /history
   Shows your last 10 delivered items.

13) /reset
   Clears stored conversation memory (only affects AI context). Preferences/favorites/history remain.

14) /admin_status (admin-only)
   Shows uptime, DB status, reminders enabled count, last reminder loop run time, and env presence booleans.

Reminders
- Implemented as a single-process loop running every 60 seconds.
- For each reminders-enabled user, it checks their local time (timezone) and sends at the scheduled minute.
- Uses lastReminderSentAt to ensure it only sends once per day.

Privacy
If MongoDB is enabled, the bot stores:
1) Your Telegram user ID
2) Selected categories
3) Reminder settings (time/timezone/type)
4) Favorites and history items (text)
5) Latest mood and timestamp

If MongoDB is not configured, the bot runs in stateless mode and disables reminders, favorites, and history.

Troubleshooting
1) Bot won’t start
- Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.

2) AI replies fail
- Ensure COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY are set.

3) Reminders don’t fire
- Ensure MONGODB_URI is set and /remind has been configured.
- Check logs for reminder loop cycles and any send failures.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Motivation & Life Tips Telegram Bot (Production-Ready Plan)

Summary:
A Telegram bot that delivers motivational messages and practical life tips in a personalized, safe, and reliable way. Users can request on-demand motivation, choose topics (habits, productivity, stress, relationships, fitness, confidence), set a daily schedule, and track streaks and preferences. The system is built as a single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence and optional filesystem/object storage for any admin-imported content assets.

The bot emphasizes user control (frequency, quiet hours, opt-out), content quality (curated tips + admin moderation), and operational robustness (rate limiting, idempotent scheduling, error handling, observability). It also supports an admin workflow for adding, reviewing, and segmenting content so the bot remains fresh without redeployments.

Product goals:
Primary user value
1) Provide fast, helpful motivation: On-demand messages that feel relevant to the user’s moment and preferences.
2) Build positive routines: Daily scheduled tips and reminders that users can tailor (time, frequency, topics).
3) Encourage small actions: Each message can include a lightweight prompt (one-minute task, reflection question) to convert motivation into behavior.

Core features
1) On-demand motivation: A single command or button produces a message immediately.
2) Topic selection: Users choose categories and can exclude topics.
3) Scheduling: Daily (or multiple times/day) delivery with quiet hours.
4) Streaks and light tracking: Track consecutive days interacted with, and optionally simple “did it” confirmations.
5) Personalization: Basic preference-based selection plus rotation to avoid repetition.
6) Safety and boundaries: Non-medical disclaimers, crisis guidance language, and content filters for sensitive topics.

Admin and content goals
1) Curated content library: Store tips/quotes/prompts with metadata, topics, language, tone, and difficulty.
2) Moderation and iteration: Admin review workflow and quick disabling of problematic content.
3) Analytics-lite: Aggregate counts (deliveries, opt-outs, topic popularity) without collecting unnecessary personal data.

Out of scope (initially)
1) Deep mental-health counseling or diagnostic advice.
2) Heavy gamification or social leaderboards that could harm privacy.
3) Complex NLP personalization; keep it rule-based for predictability.

High-level architecture:
High-level design (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram update handling: grammY receives updates via webhook or long polling. The bot routes updates through middleware for logging, rate limiting, and user session loading.
2) Core modules:
   1) Conversation/UI module: Menus and flows for onboarding, topic selection, scheduling, and feedback.
   2) Content selection engine: Picks the next tip based on user preferences, recent history, and content availability.
   3) Scheduler: A periodic in-process job that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and sends messages. It must be idempotent and safe across retries.
   4) Admin tools: Commands for ingesting content, approving content, and viewing basic stats.
   5) Safety and policy layer: Filters and disclaimers, plus a simple “not a substitute for professional help” footer for certain categories.

Request path and middleware
1) Inbound updates go through:
   1) Correlation ID assignment for logs.
   2) Basic rate limiting per user/chat to reduce spam and accidental loops.
   3) User record loading/creation in MongoDB.
   4) Locale and preference resolution.
   5) Command and callback routing.
2) Outbound messages go through:
   1) Send wrapper with retries for transient errors.
   2) Telegram error classification (blocked bot, chat not found) to auto-disable scheduling for that chat.

Scheduling approach (no extra infra)
1) A timer (for example every 30–60 seconds) runs a “due deliveries” scan.
2) It finds user schedules whose nextDeliveryAt is in the past, attempts to send, and then updates nextDeliveryAt.
3) Idempotency: Each scheduled send is recorded as a delivery event with a unique key (userId + scheduleId + deliveryWindow) so that restarts do not create duplicates.
4) Backoff: If Telegram fails due to rate limits or transient errors, reschedule with a short delay and track attempt counts.

Reliability boundaries
1) The service remains single-process as required; durability comes from MongoDB.
2) All “state transitions” (e.g., schedule advanced, content marked as used) are persisted so a restart continues correctly.
3) If MongoDB is down, the bot can still respond minimally (inform user) but should avoid losing scheduling state; it can temporarily disable scheduled sends until persistence is restored.

Data model:
User collection
1) userId: Telegram user ID
2) chatId: chat to deliver messages (private chat default)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) status: active, paused, blocked
5) locale: e.g., en
6) preferences:
   1) topicsIncluded: list
   2) topicsExcluded: list
   3) tone: uplifting, practical, tough-love (optional)
   4) messageLength: short, medium (optional)
7) quietHours:
   1) startLocalTime, endLocalTime
   2) timezoneOffsetMinutes or inferred timezone mode
8) streak:
   1) currentCount
   2) lastInteractionDate (date only in user’s local notion)
9) deliveryHistory (lightweight pointers): lastNContentIds or a rolling hash to reduce repetition

Schedules collection
1) scheduleId
2) userId, chatId
3) enabled: boolean
4) frequency:
   1) dailyAtLocalTime or multipleTimes array
   2) daysOfWeek optional
5) nextDeliveryAt: UTC timestamp
6) lastDeliveredAt
7) lastDeliveryContentId
8) failureState:
   1) consecutiveFailures
   2) lastFailureReason

Content collection
1) contentId
2) type: tip, quote, prompt, micro-challenge
3) text: primary message
4) topics: list
5) tone, length
6) language
7) safetyTags: e.g., mental-health-sensitive, medical
8) status: draft, approved, disabled
9) author/source metadata (optional) for attribution
10) createdAt, updatedAt

DeliveryEvents collection (for idempotency and audit)
1) eventId
2) userId, scheduleId
3) deliveryKey: unique string
4) contentId
5) status: sent, failed, skipped
6) attemptedAt, sentAt
7) telegramMessageId (if sent)

Feedback collection (optional but useful)
1) userId, contentId
2) rating: like, dislike
3) freeformReason (optional)
4) createdAt

Admin collection (or config)
1) adminUserIds list in environment/config
2) content import logs and actions (optional).

Key flows:
Onboarding flow
1) User starts with /start.
2) Bot explains what it does, asks for topic preferences (buttons).
3) Bot asks if user wants daily motivation and at what time.
4) Bot confirms quiet hours default and how to change later.
5) Bot sends a sample message and offers “More like this / Less like this” feedback.

On-demand motivation
1) User runs /motivate or taps “Motivate me”.
2) Selection engine filters approved content by language, included/excluded topics, and tone.
3) De-duplication: avoid last N items delivered to that user.
4) Bot sends message with quick feedback buttons:
   1) Another one
   2) Save
   3) Not for me
   4) Change topics

Daily scheduled delivery
1) Scheduler finds due schedules.
2) For each schedule:
   1) Check user status active.
   2) Check quiet hours; if within quiet hours, move nextDeliveryAt to end of quiet window.
   3) Select content and create a DeliveryEvent with unique deliveryKey.
   4) Send message; on success, mark event sent and advance schedule.
   5) On known hard failures (bot blocked), set user status blocked and disable schedule.

Streak update
1) On any meaningful interaction (requesting a tip, giving feedback), update lastInteractionDate.
2) If lastInteractionDate is yesterday (user-local), increment streak; otherwise reset to 1.
3) Optionally show streak milestones sparingly to avoid annoyance.

Feedback loop
1) Like: increases weight for that topic/tone and can add contentId to a “favorites” list.
2) Dislike: mark contentId as “avoid for this user” and optionally reduce topic weight.
3) If user repeatedly dislikes a topic, prompt to adjust preferences.

Admin content management
1) Admin adds content via command (text pasted or uploaded file parsed externally by admin and inserted manually).
2) Content enters draft status.
3) Admin reviews and approves; only approved content is eligible.
4) Admin can disable content immediately if issues arise.
5) Admin can request basic stats: deliveries today, top topics, failure counts.

Safety handling
1) If user asks for mental health crisis help, bot responds with a safe, non-clinical message encouraging contacting local emergency services or trusted contacts.
2) For sensitive tags, append a brief disclaimer and avoid prescriptive medical claims.
3) Provide /stop and /pause for control, always respected.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js using ESM modules.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.
3) A single Node.js service/process for all update handling and scheduling.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for user profiles, schedules, content library, delivery events, and feedback.
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (e.g., recent rate-limit buckets, short-lived session hints), never as the source of truth.

Scheduling
1) In-process interval-based scheduler that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and updates schedule state transactionally where possible.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Storage for assets (optional)
1) If later supporting images/audio: store files in simple filesystem storage or object storage.
2) Store only file references in MongoDB.

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) MongoDB connection string and admin user IDs from environment variables.
3) Feature flags via environment variables (e.g., enable daily tips, enable feedback collection).

Deployment
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Use webhook mode if the environment supports inbound HTTPS, otherwise long polling.
3) Graceful shutdown handling to finish in-flight sends and stop scheduler cleanly.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent scheduling via DeliveryEvents so restarts do not duplicate scheduled messages.
2) Defensive sending: classify Telegram API errors, disable schedules for blocked chats, and retry only when appropriate.
3) Data integrity: ensure schedule updates and delivery events are consistent; prefer atomic updates per schedule.

Performance
1) Batch due-schedule queries and limit concurrency of outbound sends to respect Telegram limits.
2) Use simple caching for content counts or topic lists, but rely on MongoDB as source of truth.

Security and privacy
1) Collect minimal user data: Telegram IDs, preferences, schedule settings, and delivery history.
2) Provide /delete to remove user data and disable schedules.
3) Avoid storing message text from users unless necessary for a feature; keep feedback structured.
4) Admin commands restricted by admin user IDs.

Observability
1) Structured logs for updates, sends, failures, and scheduler cycles.
2) Basic metrics counters (even if only logged): sends attempted, sends succeeded, failures by type, opt-outs.
3) Error tracking integration can be added conceptually, but keep implementation within the single service boundaries.

Content quality and compliance
1) Maintain an approval workflow for new content.
2) Add guardrails: no hate, harassment, medical claims, or targeted personal advice.
3) Localization-ready content fields to allow adding more languages later.

User experience
1) Respect quiet hours and allow easy pause/stop.
2) Keep messages short by default; allow users to opt into longer tips.
3) Use buttons for common actions to reduce typing.

Operational playbook
1) Back up MongoDB regularly.
2) Have an emergency kill switch to disable scheduled sends (environment flag) if needed.
3) Provide admin commands to pause all schedules or disable a content category quickly.

Bot commands:
• /start — Begin onboarding and set initial preferences for motivation topics and delivery schedule. (usage: /start) [user]
• /motivate — Get an instant motivational message or practical life tip based on your preferences. (usage: /motivate) [user]
• /topics — Choose which topics you want more or less of (habits, productivity, stress, confidence, etc.). (usage: /topics) [user]
• /schedule — Configure daily delivery time and frequency, and enable/disable scheduled messages. (usage: /schedule) [user]
• /pause — Pause scheduled motivation without deleting settings. (usage: /pause) [user]
• /resume — Resume scheduled motivation after pausing. (usage: /resume) [user]
• /quiet — Set quiet hours when the bot should not send scheduled messages. (usage: /quiet) [user]
• /streak — View your current engagement streak and recent activity. (usage: /streak) [user]
• /feedback — Adjust what you receive by rating the last message or setting preferences. (usage: /feedback) [user]
• /help — Show help, tips on using the bot, and safety guidance for sensitive situations. (usage: /help) [user]
• /stop — Stop all scheduled messages and opt out. (usage: /stop) [user]
• /delete — Delete your stored data (preferences, schedules, history) and disable the bot for you. (usage: /delete) [user]
• /adminaddcontent — Add a new motivational tip/quote/prompt to the content library (admin only). (usage: /adminaddcontent) [admin]
• /adminreview — List draft content awaiting approval and approve/disable items (admin only). (usage: /adminreview) [admin]
• /adminstats — View basic operational stats such as sends today and failure counts (admin only). (usage: /adminstats) [admin]
• /adminpauseall — Emergency switch to pause all scheduled deliveries (admin only). (usage: /adminpauseall) [admin]

planSections.productGoals:
Primary user value
1) Provide fast, helpful motivation: On-demand messages that feel relevant to the user’s moment and preferences.
2) Build positive routines: Daily scheduled tips and reminders that users can tailor (time, frequency, topics).
3) Encourage small actions: Each message can include a lightweight prompt (one-minute task, reflection question) to convert motivation into behavior.

Core features
1) On-demand motivation: A single command or button produces a message immediately.
2) Topic selection: Users choose categories and can exclude topics.
3) Scheduling: Daily (or multiple times/day) delivery with quiet hours.
4) Streaks and light tracking: Track consecutive days interacted with, and optionally simple “did it” confirmations.
5) Personalization: Basic preference-based selection plus rotation to avoid repetition.
6) Safety and boundaries: Non-medical disclaimers, crisis guidance language, and content filters for sensitive topics.

Admin and content goals
1) Curated content library: Store tips/quotes/prompts with metadata, topics, language, tone, and difficulty.
2) Moderation and iteration: Admin review workflow and quick disabling of problematic content.
3) Analytics-lite: Aggregate counts (deliveries, opt-outs, topic popularity) without collecting unnecessary personal data.

Out of scope (initially)
1) Deep mental-health counseling or diagnostic advice.
2) Heavy gamification or social leaderboards that could harm privacy.
3) Complex NLP personalization; keep it rule-based for predictability.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
High-level design (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram update handling: grammY receives updates via webhook or long polling. The bot routes updates through middleware for logging, rate limiting, and user session loading.
2) Core modules:
   1) Conversation/UI module: Menus and flows for onboarding, topic selection, scheduling, and feedback.
   2) Content selection engine: Picks the next tip based on user preferences, recent history, and content availability.
   3) Scheduler: A periodic in-process job that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and sends messages. It must be idempotent and safe across retries.
   4) Admin tools: Commands for ingesting content, approving content, and viewing basic stats.
   5) Safety and policy layer: Filters and disclaimers, plus a simple “not a substitute for professional help” footer for certain categories.

Request path and middleware
1) Inbound updates go through:
   1) Correlation ID assignment for logs.
   2) Basic rate limiting per user/chat to reduce spam and accidental loops.
   3) User record loading/creation in MongoDB.
   4) Locale and preference resolution.
   5) Command and callback routing.
2) Outbound messages go through:
   1) Send wrapper with retries for transient errors.
   2) Telegram error classification (blocked bot, chat not found) to auto-disable scheduling for that chat.

Scheduling approach (no extra infra)
1) A timer (for example every 30–60 seconds) runs a “due deliveries” scan.
2) It finds user schedules whose nextDeliveryAt is in the past, attempts to send, and then updates nextDeliveryAt.
3) Idempotency: Each scheduled send is recorded as a delivery event with a unique key (userId + scheduleId + deliveryWindow) so that restarts do not create duplicates.
4) Backoff: If Telegram fails due to rate limits or transient errors, reschedule with a short delay and track attempt counts.

Reliability boundaries
1) The service remains single-process as required; durability comes from MongoDB.
2) All “state transitions” (e.g., schedule advanced, content marked as used) are persisted so a restart continues correctly.
3) If MongoDB is down, the bot can still respond minimally (inform user) but should avoid losing scheduling state; it can temporarily disable scheduled sends until persistence is restored.
planSections.dataModel:
User collection
1) userId: Telegram user ID
2) chatId: chat to deliver messages (private chat default)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) status: active, paused, blocked
5) locale: e.g., en
6) preferences:
   1) topicsIncluded: list
   2) topicsExcluded: list
   3) tone: uplifting, practical, tough-love (optional)
   4) messageLength: short, medium (optional)
7) quietHours:
   1) startLocalTime, endLocalTime
   2) timezoneOffsetMinutes or inferred timezone mode
8) streak:
   1) currentCount
   2) lastInteractionDate (date only in user’s local notion)
9) deliveryHistory (lightweight pointers): lastNContentIds or a rolling hash to reduce repetition

Schedules collection
1) scheduleId
2) userId, chatId
3) enabled: boolean
4) frequency:
   1) dailyAtLocalTime or multipleTimes array
   2) daysOfWeek optional
5) nextDeliveryAt: UTC timestamp
6) lastDeliveredAt
7) lastDeliveryContentId
8) failureState:
   1) consecutiveFailures
   2) lastFailureReason

Content collection
1) contentId
2) type: tip, quote, prompt, micro-challenge
3) text: primary message
4) topics: list
5) tone, length
6) language
7) safetyTags: e.g., mental-health-sensitive, medical
8) status: draft, approved, disabled
9) author/source metadata (optional) for attribution
10) createdAt, updatedAt

DeliveryEvents collection (for idempotency and audit)
1) eventId
2) userId, scheduleId
3) deliveryKey: unique string
4) contentId
5) status: sent, failed, skipped
6) attemptedAt, sentAt
7) telegramMessageId (if sent)

Feedback collection (optional but useful)
1) userId, contentId
2) rating: like, dislike
3) freeformReason (optional)
4) createdAt

Admin collection (or config)
1) adminUserIds list in environment/config
2) content import logs and actions (optional).
planSections.keyFlows:
Onboarding flow
1) User starts with /start.
2) Bot explains what it does, asks for topic preferences (buttons).
3) Bot asks if user wants daily motivation and at what time.
4) Bot confirms quiet hours default and how to change later.
5) Bot sends a sample message and offers “More like this / Less like this” feedback.

On-demand motivation
1) User runs /motivate or taps “Motivate me”.
2) Selection engine filters approved content by language, included/excluded topics, and tone.
3) De-duplication: avoid last N items delivered to that user.
4) Bot sends message with quick feedback buttons:
   1) Another one
   2) Save
   3) Not for me
   4) Change topics

Daily scheduled delivery
1) Scheduler finds due schedules.
2) For each schedule:
   1) Check user status active.
   2) Check quiet hours; if within quiet hours, move nextDeliveryAt to end of quiet window.
   3) Select content and create a DeliveryEvent with unique deliveryKey.
   4) Send message; on success, mark event sent and advance schedule.
   5) On known hard failures (bot blocked), set user status blocked and disable schedule.

Streak update
1) On any meaningful interaction (requesting a tip, giving feedback), update lastInteractionDate.
2) If lastInteractionDate is yesterday (user-local), increment streak; otherwise reset to 1.
3) Optionally show streak milestones sparingly to avoid annoyance.

Feedback loop
1) Like: increases weight for that topic/tone and can add contentId to a “favorites” list.
2) Dislike: mark contentId as “avoid for this user” and optionally reduce topic weight.
3) If user repeatedly dislikes a topic, prompt to adjust preferences.

Admin content management
1) Admin adds content via command (text pasted or uploaded file parsed externally by admin and inserted manually).
2) Content enters draft status.
3) Admin reviews and approves; only approved content is eligible.
4) Admin can disable content immediately if issues arise.
5) Admin can request basic stats: deliveries today, top topics, failure counts.

Safety handling
1) If user asks for mental health crisis help, bot responds with a safe, non-clinical message encouraging contacting local emergency services or trusted contacts.
2) For sensitive tags, append a brief disclaimer and avoid prescriptive medical claims.
3) Provide /stop and /pause for control, always respected.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js using ESM modules.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.
3) A single Node.js service/process for all update handling and scheduling.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for user profiles, schedules, content library, delivery events, and feedback.
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (e.g., recent rate-limit buckets, short-lived session hints), never as the source of truth.

Scheduling
1) In-process interval-based scheduler that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and updates schedule state transactionally where possible.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Storage for assets (optional)
1) If later supporting images/audio: store files in simple filesystem storage or object storage.
2) Store only file references in MongoDB.

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) MongoDB connection string and admin user IDs from environment variables.
3) Feature flags via environment variables (e.g., enable daily tips, enable feedback collection).

Deployment
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Use webhook mode if the environment supports inbound HTTPS, otherwise long polling.
3) Graceful shutdown handling to finish in-flight sends and stop scheduler cleanly.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent scheduling via DeliveryEvents so restarts do not duplicate scheduled messages.
2) Defensive sending: classify Telegram API errors, disable schedules for blocked chats, and retry only when appropriate.
3) Data integrity: ensure schedule updates and delivery events are consistent; prefer atomic updates per schedule.

Performance
1) Batch due-schedule queries and limit concurrency of outbound sends to respect Telegram limits.
2) Use simple caching for content counts or topic lists, but rely on MongoDB as source of truth.

Security and privacy
1) Collect minimal user data: Telegram IDs, preferences, schedule settings, and delivery history.
2) Provide /delete to remove user data and disable schedules.
3) Avoid storing message text from users unless necessary for a feature; keep feedback structured.
4) Admin commands restricted by admin user IDs.

Observability
1) Structured logs for updates, sends, failures, and scheduler cycles.
2) Basic metrics counters (even if only logged): sends attempted, sends succeeded, failures by type, opt-outs.
3) Error tracking integration can be added conceptually, but keep implementation within the single service boundaries.

Content quality and compliance
1) Maintain an approval workflow for new content.
2) Add guardrails: no hate, harassment, medical claims, or targeted personal advice.
3) Localization-ready content fields to allow adding more languages later.

User experience
1) Respect quiet hours and allow easy pause/stop.
2) Keep messages short by default; allow users to opt into longer tips.
3) Use buttons for common actions to reduce typing.

Operational playbook
1) Back up MongoDB regularly.
2) Have an emergency kill switch to disable scheduled sends (environment flag) if needed.
3) Provide admin commands to pause all schedules or disable a content category quickly.

DOCS.md.content:
This bot provides motivational messages and practical life tips tailored to a user’s chosen categories, with optional daily reminders.

Setup
1) Install dependencies
Run: npm run install:root

2) Configure environment
Copy .env.sample to .env and set at least TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN.

3) Run
Development: npm run dev
Production: npm run build then npm start

Public commands
1) /start
Starts onboarding.
It asks you to pick categories (multi-select) and whether you want daily reminders.

2) /help
Shows available commands and short examples.

3) /motivate
Generates a short motivational message tailored to your selected categories and recent mood (if set).
Buttons: Save, Another, Change categories.

4) /tip
Generates a practical, concise tip tailored to your categories.
It includes 2–4 steps.
Buttons: Save, Another, Change categories.

5) /mood
Lets you select a mood (Great, Okay, Stressed, Sad, Unmotivated, Angry, Anxious).
The bot stores your latest mood and replies with a message in that tone.

6) /categories
Shows your current selected categories and lets you toggle them.

7) /remind
Configures reminders.
Flow: enter time (HH:MM) then timezone (IANA like Europe/London, default UTC), then reminder type (Motivation, Tip, Mixed).

8) /remind_off
Turns off reminders.

9) /save
Saves the last delivered motivation/tip item.

10) /favorites
Lists your saved items with pagination.
Each item has a Remove button.

11) /remove_favorite
If invoked as a command, it guides you to use /favorites for removal.

12) /history
Shows your last 10 delivered items with a short snippet and timestamp.

13) /reset
Clears the conversation memory used for AI context (if DB is enabled). It does not change your preferences.

14) /admin_status (admin-only)
Shows diagnostics: uptime, DB connected, reminders enabled count, last reminder loop run time, env presence booleans.
If BOT_ADMINS is not set, the command is disabled.

Environment variables
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN (required)
Telegram bot token.

2) COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT (required for AI)
Base URL for CookMyBots AI Gateway. Example: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai

3) COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY (required for AI)
API key for the CookMyBots AI Gateway.

4) MONGODB_URI (optional but recommended)
Enables stored preferences, reminders, favorites, and history.
If not set, the bot runs in stateless mode with defaults and disables persistence features.

5) BOT_ADMINS (optional)
Comma-separated Telegram user IDs allowed to run /admin_status.
If missing, admin commands are disabled.

Reminder behavior
The reminder loop runs every 60 seconds in the same process.
It checks each reminders-enabled user’s local time based on their configured timezone.
It sends once per day per scheduled minute using a lastReminderSentAt field to prevent duplicates.

AI safety notes
The bot is supportive and practical.
It avoids moralizing, shame, and absolute claims.
It does not provide medical or legal advice.
If the user expresses potential self-harm or severe crisis, the bot responds with a supportive message encouraging contacting local emergency services or a trusted person.

Troubleshooting
1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN missing
The bot exits with a clear message. Set it and restart.

2) Mongo not configured
Preferences/reminders/favorites/history are disabled. The bot will tell the user when they try to use those features.

3) AI not configured
/motivate and /tip will explain that AI keys are missing.

4) Conflicting polling instances
If Telegram returns a 409 conflict, the bot backs off and retries automatically.

README.md.content:
This repo contains a Telegram bot (grammY) that delivers motivational messages and practical life tips.

Features
1) Personalized categories (multi-select)
2) /motivate and /tip generation via CookMyBots AI Gateway
3) Mood selection (/mood) to adjust tone
4) Favorites (save/remove + pagination)
5) History (last 10 delivered items)
6) Optional daily reminders (single-process polling loop)
7) Admin diagnostics (/admin_status)

Architecture
1) src/index.js boots config, starts the bot via long polling, and starts the reminders loop.
2) src/bot.js wires commands first, then the agent features (no catch-all chat).
3) src/commands/* contains public commands.
4) src/features/* contains callback handlers and reminders loop.
5) src/lib/* contains config, DB, AI gateway client, logging helpers.

Setup
1) Install
   npm run install:root

2) Configure env
   Copy .env.sample to .env and fill in values.

Required
- TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN

Required for AI generation
- COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT (base URL, example: https://api.cookmybots.com/api/ai)
- COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY

Optional but recommended (enables preferences, reminders, favorites, history)
- MONGODB_URI

Optional admin
- BOT_ADMINS (comma-separated Telegram user IDs)

Run
1) Dev
   npm run dev

2) Prod
   npm run build
   npm start

Commands
1) /start
   Starts onboarding: pick categories, then optionally configure reminders.

2) /help
   Shows command list and examples.

3) /motivate
   Generates a short motivational message tailored to your categories and mood. Includes buttons Save, Another, Change categories.

4) /tip
   Generates a practical tip with 2–4 steps tailored to your categories. Includes buttons Save, Another, Change categories.

5) /mood
   Pick your current mood via buttons; bot stores it and responds in that tone.

6) /categories
   View and toggle category preferences.

7) /remind
   Configure reminders: time (HH:MM), timezone (IANA, defaults to UTC), and reminder type (Motivation/Tip/Mixed).

8) /remind_off
   Disable reminders.

9) /save
   Saves the last delivered item.

10) /favorites
   Lists saved items with pagination and Remove buttons.

11) /remove_favorite
   Guides you to remove items via /favorites.

12) /history
   Shows your last 10 delivered items.

13) /reset
   Clears stored conversation memory (only affects AI context). Preferences/favorites/history remain.

14) /admin_status (admin-only)
   Shows uptime, DB status, reminders enabled count, last reminder loop run time, and env presence booleans.

Reminders
- Implemented as a single-process loop running every 60 seconds.
- For each reminders-enabled user, it checks their local time (timezone) and sends at the scheduled minute.
- Uses lastReminderSentAt to ensure it only sends once per day.

Privacy
If MongoDB is enabled, the bot stores:
1) Your Telegram user ID
2) Selected categories
3) Reminder settings (time/timezone/type)
4) Favorites and history items (text)
5) Latest mood and timestamp

If MongoDB is not configured, the bot runs in stateless mode and disables reminders, favorites, and history.

Troubleshooting
1) Bot won’t start
- Ensure TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set.

2) AI replies fail
- Ensure COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT and COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY are set.

3) Reminders don’t fire
- Ensure MONGODB_URI is set and /remind has been configured.
- Check logs for reminder loop cycles and any send failures.

export function buildBotProfile() {
  return [
    "Purpose: Provide motivational messages and practical life tips tailored to the user’s selected categories and (optionally) their current mood. The bot can also send optional daily reminders.",
    "",
    "Public commands:",
    "/start - Welcome and onboarding (choose categories + reminders)",
    "/help - Command list and examples",
    "/motivate - Get a short motivational message tailored to your categories and mood",
    "/tip - Get a practical tip tailored to your categories (with 2–4 steps)",
    "/mood - Set your current mood via buttons",
    "/categories - View and change selected categories",
    "/remind - Configure daily reminders (time, timezone, type)",
    "/remind_off - Disable reminders",
    "/save - Save the last delivered motivation/tip",
    "/favorites - List saved items and remove them",
    "/remove_favorite - Guidance for removing favorites (use /favorites)",
    "/history - Show recent delivered items",
    "/reset - Clear AI chat context memory",
    "",
    "Key rules:",
    "1) Supportive tone. Avoid moralizing, shame, or absolute claims.",
    "2) No medical or legal advice. Stress/anxiety coping tips are non-clinical.",
    "3) If a message suggests self-harm or crisis, respond supportively and encourage contacting local emergency services or a trusted person.",
    "4) Admin-only: /admin_status is restricted to BOT_ADMINS.",
    "5) Privacy: If MongoDB is enabled, the bot stores preferences, reminder settings, favorites, history, and latest mood. If MongoDB is not enabled, it runs stateless and disables those features.",
  ].join("\n");
}

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Motivation & Life Tips Telegram Bot (Production-Ready Plan)

Summary:
A Telegram bot that delivers motivational messages and practical life tips in a personalized, safe, and reliable way. Users can request on-demand motivation, choose topics (habits, productivity, stress, relationships, fitness, confidence), set a daily schedule, and track streaks and preferences. The system is built as a single Node.js (ESM) process using grammY, with MongoDB for persistence and optional filesystem/object storage for any admin-imported content assets.

The bot emphasizes user control (frequency, quiet hours, opt-out), content quality (curated tips + admin moderation), and operational robustness (rate limiting, idempotent scheduling, error handling, observability). It also supports an admin workflow for adding, reviewing, and segmenting content so the bot remains fresh without redeployments.

Product goals:
Primary user value
1) Provide fast, helpful motivation: On-demand messages that feel relevant to the user’s moment and preferences.
2) Build positive routines: Daily scheduled tips and reminders that users can tailor (time, frequency, topics).
3) Encourage small actions: Each message can include a lightweight prompt (one-minute task, reflection question) to convert motivation into behavior.

Core features
1) On-demand motivation: A single command or button produces a message immediately.
2) Topic selection: Users choose categories and can exclude topics.
3) Scheduling: Daily (or multiple times/day) delivery with quiet hours.
4) Streaks and light tracking: Track consecutive days interacted with, and optionally simple “did it” confirmations.
5) Personalization: Basic preference-based selection plus rotation to avoid repetition.
6) Safety and boundaries: Non-medical disclaimers, crisis guidance language, and content filters for sensitive topics.

Admin and content goals
1) Curated content library: Store tips/quotes/prompts with metadata, topics, language, tone, and difficulty.
2) Moderation and iteration: Admin review workflow and quick disabling of problematic content.
3) Analytics-lite: Aggregate counts (deliveries, opt-outs, topic popularity) without collecting unnecessary personal data.

Out of scope (initially)
1) Deep mental-health counseling or diagnostic advice.
2) Heavy gamification or social leaderboards that could harm privacy.
3) Complex NLP personalization; keep it rule-based for predictability.

High-level architecture:
High-level design (single Node.js service)
1) Telegram update handling: grammY receives updates via webhook or long polling. The bot routes updates through middleware for logging, rate limiting, and user session loading.
2) Core modules:
   1) Conversation/UI module: Menus and flows for onboarding, topic selection, scheduling, and feedback.
   2) Content selection engine: Picks the next tip based on user preferences, recent history, and content availability.
   3) Scheduler: A periodic in-process job that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and sends messages. It must be idempotent and safe across retries.
   4) Admin tools: Commands for ingesting content, approving content, and viewing basic stats.
   5) Safety and policy layer: Filters and disclaimers, plus a simple “not a substitute for professional help” footer for certain categories.

Request path and middleware
1) Inbound updates go through:
   1) Correlation ID assignment for logs.
   2) Basic rate limiting per user/chat to reduce spam and accidental loops.
   3) User record loading/creation in MongoDB.
   4) Locale and preference resolution.
   5) Command and callback routing.
2) Outbound messages go through:
   1) Send wrapper with retries for transient errors.
   2) Telegram error classification (blocked bot, chat not found) to auto-disable scheduling for that chat.

Scheduling approach (no extra infra)
1) A timer (for example every 30–60 seconds) runs a “due deliveries” scan.
2) It finds user schedules whose nextDeliveryAt is in the past, attempts to send, and then updates nextDeliveryAt.
3) Idempotency: Each scheduled send is recorded as a delivery event with a unique key (userId + scheduleId + deliveryWindow) so that restarts do not create duplicates.
4) Backoff: If Telegram fails due to rate limits or transient errors, reschedule with a short delay and track attempt counts.

Reliability boundaries
1) The service remains single-process as required; durability comes from MongoDB.
2) All “state transitions” (e.g., schedule advanced, content marked as used) are persisted so a restart continues correctly.
3) If MongoDB is down, the bot can still respond minimally (inform user) but should avoid losing scheduling state; it can temporarily disable scheduled sends until persistence is restored.

Data model:
User collection
1) userId: Telegram user ID
2) chatId: chat to deliver messages (private chat default)
3) createdAt, updatedAt
4) status: active, paused, blocked
5) locale: e.g., en
6) preferences:
   1) topicsIncluded: list
   2) topicsExcluded: list
   3) tone: uplifting, practical, tough-love (optional)
   4) messageLength: short, medium (optional)
7) quietHours:
   1) startLocalTime, endLocalTime
   2) timezoneOffsetMinutes or inferred timezone mode
8) streak:
   1) currentCount
   2) lastInteractionDate (date only in user’s local notion)
9) deliveryHistory (lightweight pointers): lastNContentIds or a rolling hash to reduce repetition

Schedules collection
1) scheduleId
2) userId, chatId
3) enabled: boolean
4) frequency:
   1) dailyAtLocalTime or multipleTimes array
   2) daysOfWeek optional
5) nextDeliveryAt: UTC timestamp
6) lastDeliveredAt
7) lastDeliveryContentId
8) failureState:
   1) consecutiveFailures
   2) lastFailureReason

Content collection
1) contentId
2) type: tip, quote, prompt, micro-challenge
3) text: primary message
4) topics: list
5) tone, length
6) language
7) safetyTags: e.g., mental-health-sensitive, medical
8) status: draft, approved, disabled
9) author/source metadata (optional) for attribution
10) createdAt, updatedAt

DeliveryEvents collection (for idempotency and audit)
1) eventId
2) userId, scheduleId
3) deliveryKey: unique string
4) contentId
5) status: sent, failed, skipped
6) attemptedAt, sentAt
7) telegramMessageId (if sent)

Feedback collection (optional but useful)
1) userId, contentId
2) rating: like, dislike
3) freeformReason (optional)
4) createdAt

Admin collection (or config)
1) adminUserIds list in environment/config
2) content import logs and actions (optional).

Key flows:
Onboarding flow
1) User starts with /start.
2) Bot explains what it does, asks for topic preferences (buttons).
3) Bot asks if user wants daily motivation and at what time.
4) Bot confirms quiet hours default and how to change later.
5) Bot sends a sample message and offers “More like this / Less like this” feedback.

On-demand motivation
1) User runs /motivate or taps “Motivate me”.
2) Selection engine filters approved content by language, included/excluded topics, and tone.
3) De-duplication: avoid last N items delivered to that user.
4) Bot sends message with quick feedback buttons:
   1) Another one
   2) Save
   3) Not for me
   4) Change topics

Daily scheduled delivery
1) Scheduler finds due schedules.
2) For each schedule:
   1) Check user status active.
   2) Check quiet hours; if within quiet hours, move nextDeliveryAt to end of quiet window.
   3) Select content and create a DeliveryEvent with unique deliveryKey.
   4) Send message; on success, mark event sent and advance schedule.
   5) On known hard failures (bot blocked), set user status blocked and disable schedule.

Streak update
1) On any meaningful interaction (requesting a tip, giving feedback), update lastInteractionDate.
2) If lastInteractionDate is yesterday (user-local), increment streak; otherwise reset to 1.
3) Optionally show streak milestones sparingly to avoid annoyance.

Feedback loop
1) Like: increases weight for that topic/tone and can add contentId to a “favorites” list.
2) Dislike: mark contentId as “avoid for this user” and optionally reduce topic weight.
3) If user repeatedly dislikes a topic, prompt to adjust preferences.

Admin content management
1) Admin adds content via command (text pasted or uploaded file parsed externally by admin and inserted manually).
2) Content enters draft status.
3) Admin reviews and approves; only approved content is eligible.
4) Admin can disable content immediately if issues arise.
5) Admin can request basic stats: deliveries today, top topics, failure counts.

Safety handling
1) If user asks for mental health crisis help, bot responds with a safe, non-clinical message encouraging contacting local emergency services or trusted contacts.
2) For sensitive tags, append a brief disclaimer and avoid prescriptive medical claims.
3) Provide /stop and /pause for control, always respected.

Tech stack:
Runtime and framework
1) Node.js using ESM modules.
2) grammY for Telegram bot API integration.
3) A single Node.js service/process for all update handling and scheduling.

Persistence
1) MongoDB for user profiles, schedules, content library, delivery events, and feedback.
2) In-memory maps allowed only for ephemeral caches (e.g., recent rate-limit buckets, short-lived session hints), never as the source of truth.

Scheduling
1) In-process interval-based scheduler that queries MongoDB for due deliveries and updates schedule state transactionally where possible.
2) No external queues or additional infrastructure.

Storage for assets (optional)
1) If later supporting images/audio: store files in simple filesystem storage or object storage.
2) Store only file references in MongoDB.

Configuration and secrets
1) TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.
2) MongoDB connection string and admin user IDs from environment variables.
3) Feature flags via environment variables (e.g., enable daily tips, enable feedback collection).

Deployment
1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud.
2) Use webhook mode if the environment supports inbound HTTPS, otherwise long polling.
3) Graceful shutdown handling to finish in-flight sends and stop scheduler cleanly.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability and correctness
1) Idempotent scheduling via DeliveryEvents so restarts do not duplicate scheduled messages.
2) Defensive sending: classify Telegram API errors, disable schedules for blocked chats, and retry only when appropriate.
3) Data integrity: ensure schedule updates and delivery events are consistent; prefer atomic updates per schedule.

Performance
1) Batch due-schedule queries and limit concurrency of outbound sends to respect Telegram limits.
2) Use simple caching for content counts or topic lists, but rely on MongoDB as source of truth.

Security and privacy
1) Collect minimal user data: Telegram IDs, preferences, schedule settings, and delivery history.
2) Provide /delete to remove user data and disable schedules.
3) Avoid storing message text from users unless necessary for a feature; keep feedback structured.
4) Admin commands restricted by admin user IDs.

Observability
1) Structured logs for updates, sends, failures, and scheduler cycles.
2) Basic metrics counters (even if only logged): sends attempted, sends succeeded, failures by type, opt-outs.
3) Error tracking integration can be added conceptually, but keep implementation within the single service boundaries.

Content quality and compliance
1) Maintain an approval workflow for new content.
2) Add guardrails: no hate, harassment, medical claims, or targeted personal advice.
3) Localization-ready content fields to allow adding more languages later.

User experience
1) Respect quiet hours and allow easy pause/stop.
2) Keep messages short by default; allow users to opt into longer tips.
3) Use buttons for common actions to reduce typing.

Operational playbook
1) Back up MongoDB regularly.
2) Have an emergency kill switch to disable scheduled sends (environment flag) if needed.
3) Provide admin commands to pause all schedules or disable a content category quickly.

Bot commands:
• /start — Begin onboarding and set initial preferences for motivation topics and delivery schedule. (usage: /start) [user]
• /motivate — Get an instant motivational message or practical life tip based on your preferences. (usage: /motivate) [user]
• /topics — Choose which topics you want more or less of (habits, productivity, stress, confidence, etc.). (usage: /topics) [user]
• /schedule — Configure daily delivery time and frequency, and enable/disable scheduled messages. (usage: /schedule) [user]
• /pause — Pause scheduled motivation without deleting settings. (usage: /pause) [user]
• /resume — Resume scheduled motivation after pausing. (usage: /resume) [user]
• /quiet — Set quiet hours when the bot should not send scheduled messages. (usage: /quiet) [user]
• /streak — View your current engagement streak and recent activity. (usage: /streak) [user]
• /feedback — Adjust what you receive by rating the last message or setting preferences. (usage: /feedback) [user]
• /help — Show help, tips on using the bot, and safety guidance for sensitive situations. (usage: /help) [user]
• /stop — Stop all scheduled messages and opt out. (usage: /stop) [user]
• /delete — Delete your stored data (preferences, schedules, history) and disable the bot for you. (usage: /delete) [user]
• /adminaddcontent — Add a new motivational tip/quote/prompt to the content library (admin only). (usage: /adminaddcontent) [admin]
• /adminreview — List draft content awaiting approval and approve/disable items (admin only). (usage: /adminreview) [admin]
• /adminstats — View basic operational stats such as sends today and failure counts (admin only). (usage: /adminstats) [admin]
• /adminpauseall — Emergency switch to pause all scheduled deliveries (admin only). (usage: /adminpauseall) [admin]

envVarDocs.title: Telegram Bot Token
envVarDocs.description: Token used by grammY to connect to Telegram.
envVarDocs.usage: Used on boot; required or the bot exits.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Create a bot with BotFather and copy the token.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Gateway Endpoint
envVarDocs.description: Base URL for the CookMyBots AI Gateway (must be a base URL, not /chat).
envVarDocs.usage: The bot calls POST {COOKMYBOTS_AI_ENDPOINT}/chat for motivation and tips.

envVarDocs.title: CookMyBots AI Gateway Key
envVarDocs.description: Authorization key for the CookMyBots AI Gateway.
envVarDocs.usage: Sent as Authorization: Bearer COOKMYBOTS_AI_KEY.

envVarDocs.title: MongoDB Connection String
envVarDocs.description: Enables stored preferences, reminders, favorites, and history.
envVarDocs.usage: If missing, the bot runs in stateless mode and disables persistence features.
envVarDocs.howToGet: From your MongoDB provider (Atlas or self-hosted).

envVarDocs.title: Bot Admin IDs
envVarDocs.description: Comma-separated list of Telegram user IDs allowed to use admin commands like /admin_status.
envVarDocs.usage: If missing, admin commands are disabled and will say so.
envVarDocs.howToGet: Get your Telegram numeric user ID (for example via @userinfobot).

envVarDocs.title: AI Timeout (ms)
envVarDocs.description: Timeout for AI Gateway requests.
envVarDocs.usage: Defaults to 600000 (10 minutes).

envVarDocs.title: AI Max Retries
envVarDocs.description: Retries for transient AI gateway/network errors.
envVarDocs.usage: Defaults to 2.
